mnemonics.title: GCP Compute Services - SCARF
mnemonics.content: SCARF: Serverless Cloud Functions, App Engine, Reserved Instances (Compute Engine). Helps remember compute options from fully managed (serverless) to infrastructure-as-a-service.

mnemonics.title: Storage Types - BICS
mnemonics.content: BICS: BigQuery (data warehouse), Cloud Storage (object), Cloud SQL (relational), Firestore (NoSQL). Covers main storage/database services.

mnemonics.title: Big Data Pipeline - PBD
mnemonics.content: PBD: Pub/Sub (messaging) → BigQuery (analysis) → Dataflow (processing). Flow of data from ingestion to processing to analytics.

mnemonics.title: IAM Roles - RRA
mnemonics.content: RRA: Roles → Resources → Actions. Remember: roles define what actions can be performed on resources.

mnemonics.title: GCP Global Services - NVC
mnemonics.content: NVC: Network (Cloud CDN), VPC, Cloud Load Balancing. These are globally distributed resources.

acronyms.usage: Primary term for Google's cloud computing services
Used when managing permissions and roles in GCP projects
When setting up isolated network environments in GCP
When discussing container orchestration on GCP
For object storage solutions - files, images, videos
For caching content globally closer to users
Cloud SQL for managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL)
Cloud Endpoints and Apigee for API management
Compute Engine VMs with GPU for ML/AI workloads
Cloud NAT for giving private instances internet access
Cloud DNS for managing domain name resolution

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted pyramid: Organization (top, one per company) → Folders (departments/teams) → Projects (applications/workloads) → Resources (individual services). Everything flows downward.
Think of VPC as your own private data center floor plan. Subnets are different rooms, firewall rules are doors with locks, routes are hallways showing how to get between rooms.
Visualize a hotel concierge (load balancer) directing guests (traffic) to the least crowded elevator (backend server), ensuring no single elevator is overloaded.
Picture a newspaper delivery system. Publishers (printing press) create messages (newspapers), topics (delivery routes) distribute them, and subscribers (homes) receive their copies.
Think of a ship (cluster) with a captain (control plane) managing containers (shipping containers) on the deck (worker nodes). Each container holds different cargo (application components).
Imagine buckets as labeled storage bins in a massive warehouse. Each bin has a unique address (bucket name), and you can put any item (file) inside with specific access rules (permissions).
Think of IAM like a movie theater ticket system: Who (principal) gets What (role) for Which resource. The ticket (role) determines which seats (permissions) they can access.

analogies.analogy: Like renting a physical computer in a data center. You have full control (root access), responsible for OS, software, and maintenance - like owning a dedicated server.
Like a fully serviced apartment. You just bring your furniture (code), everything else (infrastructure, scaling, maintenance) is handled by the building management (Google).
Like a vending machine. Insert money (trigger), get your snack (execute function), pay only for that specific transaction - no long-term rental costs.
Like a massive library with a super-fast search system. You don't manage the books (data), librarians (Google) handle storage and organization, you just query what you need instantly.
Like a cloud-based file cabinet. You store files (objects) in folders (buckets), access them from anywhere, and pay for the storage space you use - like Google Drive for enterprises.
Like your own private office within a large building. Even though other companies share the building (Google's infrastructure), your space is isolated and secure.
Like a traffic police officer at a busy intersection. They direct cars (requests) to different roads (servers) to prevent congestion and ensure smooth traffic flow.
Like a company HR system. Employees (principals) get job roles (roles) that determine which offices (resources) they can enter and what they can do there.
Like a shipping container management system. Containers (standardized packages) can be moved anywhere, and Kubernetes orchestrates loading/unloading across ships (servers).
Like renting a fully managed database server. Google handles backups, patches, and maintenance - you just use the database like it's your own.
Like a news agency. Publishers send stories to topics, and subscribers choose which categories they want to receive - asynchronous communication between services.
Like having copies of frequently accessed books in local library branches across a city, instead of everyone traveling to the central library. Faster access for users.
